title: The normal distribution
summary: 9709 P5 normal distribution — probability density, standardisation, inverse normal, and the 68–95–99.7 rule with GeoGebra bell curve explorer.
learningObjectives: Recognise the normal distribution X ~ N(μ, σ²) and its symmetry.
Use normal tables or calculators to find probabilities.
Solve problems involving inverse normal and given probabilities.

simpleExplanation.title: The bell curve
simpleExplanation.summary: Many natural measurements are approximately normal. The curve is symmetric about μ; σ controls spread. Standardise to Z ~ N(0,1) to use tables.
steps: Sketch the bell curve — total area = 1; symmetry about μ.
Standardise: Z = (X − μ)/σ converts any normal to standard normal.
Use tables for P(Z < z) — for P(Z > z) use 1 − Φ(z).
For P(a < X < b), standardise both ends and subtract cumulative probabilities.

flashcards.back: X ~ N(μ, σ²) — mean μ, variance σ².

sections.content: The **normal distribution** dominates Statistics papers. Cambridge tests **standardisation**, **inverse normal** (finding μ or σ from probabilities), and **continuity correction** when approximating binomial.
Z = (X − μ)/σ | P(a < X < b) = Φ((b−μ)/σ) − Φ((a−μ)/σ) | Continuity: P(X = r) ≈ P(r − 0.5 < Y < r + 0.5)
Draw a **sketch** with μ and the target value shaded — it prevents sign errors when finding tail probabilities or using symmetry P(Z < −a) = P(Z > a).

steps.caption: X ~ N(μ, σ²) is symmetric about the mean μ.
steps.embedHint: Move μ — the bell curve shifts left or right.

steps.caption: Standard deviation σ controls spread — larger σ → wider curve.
steps.embedHint: Increase σ — more probability in the tails.

steps.caption: Standardise: Z = (X − μ)/σ ~ N(0, 1) for table lookups.
steps.embedHint: Mark μ ± σ, μ ± 2σ on the curve (~68%, ~95% rules).

steps.caption: Normal approximation to binomial when np > 5 and n(1−p) > 5.
steps.embedHint: Compare bell curve area with probability statements P(X > a).
